text:
1
v  An Automated Driving System (ADS) is an integrated system
5 within vehicles that utilises various in-vehicle technologies and
3
sensors to navigate autonomously from a starting point to a
4
predefined destination. Due to its significant contribution to
7
1 safety and overall economic growth, leading car companies
.
have heavily invested in several Autonomous Vehicle (AV)
5
technologies that are able to improve safety, efficiency, and
0
4 cost savings. The technology is evolving through inter-industry
2 alliances and technological contributions across the globe.
:
Famous technological firms and car manufacturers have
v
i recently invested billions of dollars in this major transformation
X of the automobile industry; several sources estimate that the
r
self-driving car market share will take between 15 and 20 years
a
to reach 25 percent globally [1]. In line with this trend and
industrial development, governments worldwide are providing
guidance that allows and encourages on-road Autonomous
Vehicle (AV) trials. For example,  the UK published a code
of practice (2019)  that  specifies certain on-road trials [2].
Subsequently,  in early 2022,  the government changed the
Highway Code to ensure the first self-driving vehicles are
introduced safely on the roads [3].
AVs have various control units that design a system to
complete all driving tasks without human intervention. Cameras,
GPS, and other sensors are connected to exchange data to
facilitate independent driving decisions [4]. Due to dynamic
road scenarios, data comes from external roadsides and other
vehicles to the on-board sensors, which need to make continu-
ous control decisions. In general, making automated driving
systems trustworthy mainly centres around this halo of data.

1
alidation in Autonomous
ritical Review
d Poonam Yadav
Thus, for the safety of the whole environment, this data must
be preserved securely and not accessible to tampering. This is
one of the most critical challenges due to the various networks
and communications in this environment. In addition, in case
of an accident, this data becomes significant to reconstruct the
event for analysis and investigation.
This  paper  aims  to  provide  an  understanding  of
data/information flow in autonomous vehicles and their support-
ing services and infrastructure. Most current surveys investigate
the ADS as a whole and explore the ADS techniques from
the system level; with a study conducted on ADS that focuses
on the data perspective. This detailed information provides a
holistic view of the autonomous vehicle’s ecosystem, which
helps design efficient and secure data storing and validation
solutions. In this survey,  the aim to explore several critical
data aspects of Autonomous Vehicles (AV) by answering the
following fundamental questions:
• What is AV?
The definition and conceptual  understanding of Au-
tonomous Vehicles to establish a clear foundation are
explored.
• What is its Structure?
The architecture and components that constitute an Au-
tonomous Vehicle are analysed, providing insight into its
intricate design.
• What is the Data Flow? The flow of data is explained
within the Autonomous Vehicle ecosystem,  from data
acquisition to processing and utilisation.
• Who Owns the AV Data?
The ownership and control dynamics surrounding the data
generated by Autonomous Vehicles are explored, including
legal and ethical considerations.
• What are the concerns and challenges?
The concerns and challenges associated with Autonomous
Vehicles are highlighted, ranging from safety and privacy
to regulation.
Section II provides a brief overview of Autonomous Driving
Systems (ADS), describing the information required to under-
stand the structure and installation of these vehicles and all
related technologies. Section III provides an overview of the
potential benefits and costs of ADS. Section IV discusses vari-
ous data management issues, with each subsection addressing
the requirements, current problems, and gaps, and presenting
solutions  for each stage of  the data  lifecycle. Section V
addresses other  related concerns,  such as safety,  security,
privacy, and ethics. Section VI considers open questions and
future work in ADS.

Here is a fixed version of your English text:
Since  the  invention of  the automobile,  there has been
continuous innovation towards autonomous vehicles (AVs).
Figure 1 briefly illustrates  the  timeline of AV evolution,
highlighting the main developments from the 1950s to 2022.
Driving assistance systems (DAS) at all levels have been and
continue to be the most critical component in AVs. The first
generation of DAS utilised sensors that assessed a car’s internal
condition. In the 1980s, Traction Control Systems (TCS) and
Anti-lock Breaking Systems (ABS) were utilised for dynamic
vehicle stabilization, as the early era of DAS. Following this,
Electronic Stability Control (ESC) was introduced in 1995 for
the same function. Additionally, in the early 1990s, sensors
that were practically classified as exteroceptive were presented
as the second generation. The most recent generation of DAS,
also known as Advanced Driver Assist Systems (ADAS), has
been developed to prevent collisions.
Autonomous refers to systems that are able to change their
behavior  in response to unanticipated events during opera-
tion [5]. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration
(NHTSA)[4] defines autonomous vehicles as "those vehicles in
which at least aspects of a safety-critical control function (e.g.,
steering, throttle, or braking) occur without direct driver input".
Vehicles, however, that provide safety warnings to drivers (e.g.,
forward crash signs) but do not perform a control function are
not considered automated.
In September 2018,  the NHTSA performed an extensive
literature review of all  the generic AV system features to
identify the attributes that define the operational design domain
(ODD). The comprehensive review resulted in 24 ADS features,
specifically describing functionality and proposed timelines
for commercial deployment across the different Society of
Automotive Engineers (SAE) International levels of driving
automation. Accordingly, the SAE’s six-level taxonomy has
become the widespread industry standard[6]:
Level 1: Driver Assistance requires the driver to remain in
control of the driving tasks, with the exception of certain
driving modes where assistance tools may take over.
Level 2: "Partial Driving Automation" refers to a driving
mode in which the vehicle system takes over certain
driving functions, such as acceleration, while the driver
remains responsible for monitoring the driving situation.
Level 3: Conditional Driving Automation. While the
vehicle system is responsible for some driving modes
and monitoring the entire environment, at this level, the
driver must be prepared to take control at all times and
be responsive to alerts.
Level 4: High Driving Automation. While the vehicle
system is designed to perform all driving tasks for specific
dynamic driving tasks, the driver may take control.
Level 5: "Full driving automation" refers to a driving
mode where the vehicle system undertakes all driving
tasks in all conditions, thereby relieving the driver of any
responsibility to monitor the environment or take control
of the vehicle.

2
A. AV Architecture
Researchers studying autonomous vehicles (AVs) focus on
two main areas: identifying their components and defining their
functional perspective. Some papers focus on the technical
aspects of AV components, while others take a functional
approach [7], [8]. From a technical perspective, AVs have two
main layers: hardware and software. Each layer is comprised
of several subcomponents. There is some disagreement among
researchers about how to categorise the core competencies of
different subsystems when defining the functional perspective
of AVs. However, in general, AV systems are made up of three
to five primary functions: perception, localisation, planning,
control and navigation, and system management [9], [10], as
illustrated in Figure 2.
1) AV Main Operations:
a) Perception refers to collecting data and extracting relevant
understanding from the environment such as the detection
of road signs, as well as object detection and classifica-
tion [11], [12].

These detection tasks are performed by
various sensors such as cameras and Radio Detection And
Ranging (RADAR).
b) Localisation refers to the ability of the AV system to
determine the vehicle’s position and orientation relative
to the environment.
c) Planning consists of three stages:
• Using algorithms, the path planner calculates the most
efficient geometric path.
• The behaviour planner determines the optimal behaviour
based on the path planned by the path planner.
• The estimation of the best possible route subject  to
vehicle dynamics and environmental constraints [7].
d) Control refers to executing planned actions and managing
the vehicle’s motions, such as changing lanes [9], [11].
e) System management includes all the functions related
to event  data  recorders,  human-machine  interactions
involving in-vehicle interfaces [9], and external human-
machine interfaces.
2) Sensors  and Sensors Fusion:  For  an AV vehicle
to have a high-quality and real-time understanding of  its
surrounding environment,  it must  quickly and accurately
detect, comprehend, and track all objects. As a result, relying
on a single source to generate all necessary data is impossible.
Multiple sensors are equipped to provide both perceptual
and location views of the environment, allowing the vehicle
to make real-time decisions [13]. Sensors are devices that
translate detected objects or  changes  in  the surrounding
environment into quantitative measurements for processing [8].
Sensors Types
AV systems, while  having  some  variations,  all  consist
of two primary types of sensors based on their operational
principle: Exteroceptive and Proprioceptive. Exteroceptive
sensors are external state sensors that are utilised to perceive
the environment, such as calculating the distance to objects or
light intensity from the surroundings of the system. Examples
of these sensors include cameras, radio detection and ranging

Fig. 1.  The advancement of autonomous vehicles in the last few decades.
Fig. 2. Autonomous Vehicle Components.
(RADAR), etc. Proprioceptive sensors, on the other hand,
are internal state sensors that capture the dynamic state and
measure the internal values of a dynamic system. Examples
of  this type of sensor  include Global Positioning Systems
(GPS),  encoders,  accelerometers,  etc.[8],  [14]. The most
commonly used sensors for autonomous vehicles are listed
in tableI. In addition, Ahangar et al. [15] provided a detailed
comparison of sensors and their  individual challenges. As
each sensor has different advantages and limitations in other
aspects, integrating sensors is necessary to obtain an optimal
perspective, and this operation is known as "sensor fusion".
Sensor Fusion
The advantage of  this operation  is  to combine  the data
originating from different sources to complete each other’s
functions in order to provide an improved outcome in some
specific criteria and data aspects for decision tasks [16]. The
best example to explain the benefit of this method is to fuse
RADAR sensors and camera images where these sensors
have different  strengths and weaknesses. RADAR is not
affected by the illumination of  the environment, but  it  is
not able to provide accurate data regarding an object’s body.
On the other side,  the Camera’s image could provide these
data, however,  it may provide conflicting data under some
illumination conditions [13]. The initial step in data fusion is
sensor calibration, which means notifying the ADS regarding
the sensors’ position and orientation to collect and associate
the data in space and time [17]. While the various sensors
capture data in relation to the same object, the produced data
could be combined to obtain different information in order to

3
achieve higher-quality output. Other explicit examples with
details and fusion techniques are discussed by Campbell et
al. [13] and Fayyad et al. [14].
B. Communication in Autonomous Vehicle
AVs are able to communicate with any compatible systems,
including other AVs, infrastructure, and pedestrians[18], [19].
This communication  is known as Vehicle  to Everything
(V2X) technology, referring how the vehicle communicates to
everything. The network must be continuously fast, reliable,
and secure to achieve an efficient cooperative environment
with minimal delay (latency). Specifically, there are two main
tendencies used all over  the world:  the wireless standard
802.11p or mobile networks, especially 5G [20], [21], [22]. In
the near future, sixth-generation (6G) wireless systems will be
crucial for V2X communications in autonomous vehicles. [23]
The differences between conventional and self-driving cars
are evident; nonetheless, the connected car is a different phrase
in-car  technology.

In the literature,  there is sometimes no
distinction made between connected and autonomous vehicles.
However, they are considered as a main step toward a fully AV.
The connected car’s concept is part of the Internet of Things
(IoT) where the vehicle exchanges data, updates software, and
connects with other vehicle devices. In this smart vehicle, all
the electronic control units ECUs and onboard units (OBUs)
are all connected across several digital buses such as Control
Area Network (CAN), Ethernet, FlexRay, Local Interconnect
Network (LIN), Media Oriented Systems Transport (MOST)
and Bluetooth [24], [25]. Such capabilities along with sensing,
communicating with the surroundings, and controlling the
driving tasks represent the AV. A connected and Automated
Vehicle (CAV) is a vehicle that performs automated driving
tasks and connectivity with other vehicles, road users, the road
infrastructure, and the cloud [26].
There is no doubt that conventional cars constitute the vast
majority on today’s roads. However, some strong expectations
state that the number of connected cars will reach 700 million
by 2030, while the number of AVs will exceed 90 million
[24]. Correspondingly, Zhang et al. [27] address the problem
of optimally controlling CAVs under mixed traffic conditions
where both CAVs and conventional are together on the roads.
1) Communication Technologies:.

Fig. 3. VANET technologies based on transmission Range [15].
• Vehicular Ad-Hoc Networks (VANETs):
these consists of a group of vehicles connected via a
wireless network [28]. This network is a basic part of the
Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) framework [18]
to assist various connections:
1) Vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication that allows
the vehicles to communicate with each other and share
the necessary information such as traffic jamming. To
establish V2V communication, vehicles should have
an On Board Unit (OBU), Omnidirectional antennas,
sensors and actuators, and a Global Positioning System
(GPS) [29].
2) Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) communication enables
the vehicles to interact with the roadside units RSUs
which are fixed devices  installed next  to the road
covering a dedicated area [29]. These communications
are mostly conducted by using wireless dedicated short-
range communications (DSRC), which aims to provide
active safety and convenience services [26].
3) Vehicle-To-Everything (V2X) Communication allows
the vehicle to communicate with other entities using
technologies such as Cellular V2X (C-V2X) including
5G and 6G.
Different VANET technologies are based on transmission
range (see Figure 3). Ahangar et al. presented a comprehensive
survey regarding different vehicle communication technologies,
their application, limitations, and advantage [15].

5G is suitable for long-range transmission to meet the high-
mobility demand architecture; while 6G is still in its conceptual-
isation phase, although the technology-driven key performance
indicates extremely serves the AV’s communication require-
ments [30].  In general, various regions prefer DSRC over
C-V2X (e.g., USA) due to the heavily deployed infrastructure
around the country, however,  this recently changed when
Europe decided to move with cellular-based technology for
CAVs [31].
In Brussels (2018),  the 5G Infrastructure Public Private
Partnership (5G PPP) which is a cooperative combined initiative
between the European Commission and the European ICT
industry (ICT manufacturers,  telecommunications operators,
service providers, SMEs, and researcher Institutions, launched
to deliver solutions, architectures, and standards for the next
generation communication infrastructures [32]. The survey
provided by Hakak et al. [33] highlights and summarises the
key projects related to the 5G autonomous vehicles.
III. AUTONOMOUS VEHICLE POTENTIAL BENEFITS AND
COSTS
Self-driving technology is a promising future in safety,
mobility,  environment,  and  luxury.  It may  reduce crash
risks by avoiding human error and distracted driving. Many
conventional vehicle crashes occur due to human error and
distracted driving. For example, partially automated crash
avoidance features could reduce  the severity of as many
as 1.3 3 million crashes in the USA every year,  including
133,000 injury crashes and 10,100 fatal crashes [34]. Thus,
traffic will disregard poor human driving conduct and improve
performance in terms of road safety and traffic congestion. In
addition, self-driving cars contribute significantly to reducing
emissions (worldwide goal to achieve net-zero by 2050 [35]).
Other benefits and advantages are summarised in Table  III.
Comparatively, even though adopting self-driving will increase
safety, there are particular safety in relation to its development.
Its total or partial dependence on driving assistance systems
results in a serious risk with both hardware and software
issues. Additionally,  sensors may be compromised due to
environmental conditions such as dangerous weather. Another
significant concern is through cyber attacks that an automobile
and/or its technological environment may be subject to causing
grave privacy and security issues. For example, Networks,

In this section,  the current open issues and solutions are
discussed in terms of data management in AV systems. This
massive collection of data moves through different paths under
varied processing and storage stages; starting from real-time
data collecting until data at rest reaches its final destination in
the backed cloud servers(see figure 4).
A. Data Collection
The vehicle hardware and software equipment generates
large amounts of data. For example, in each second, cameras
generate 20-40 MB, and LiDAR generates 10-70 MB data [36].
The complexity of the AV systems begins with the multiple
data sources which have different data types and formats that
the AVS needs to deal with. In Figure 5 these data sources
are illustrated, which can be summarised as follows:
1) Data about the owner (Number 1 in the Figure)
2) Data comes from the vehicle’s status; for example, if the
tank or battery is almost empty. (Number 1 in the Figure)
3) Data comes from the vehicle itself by its sensors. (Number
1 in the Figure)
4) Data from other vehicles presenting their surroundings,
such as other vehicles sharing information about traffic
jams or accidents. That also includes data from smart road
units or traffic lights in smart cities.
(Number 2, 3 ,4 and 5,  respectively; V2I vehicle to
infrastructure; V2V vehicle to vehicle; V2P vehicle to

5
Fig. 5. Data Sources in AV.
pedestrian; V2X vehicle to everything; and V2N vehicle
to network communications)
The collected data feeds the onboard diagnostics which are
part of the ADS function, i.e. driving decision-making, analysis
of crashes, or technical failures. However, a privacy breach
is a serious challenge for the vehicle owner, producers,  the
public, and lawmakers. Autonomous vehicles may track the
owner or passengers of the vehicle, including settings, such as
synced contacts, addresses entered into the navigation system,
fingerprints, facial recognition, etc. In addition, sensors on AVs
allow them to gather data regarding the whole environment
of  the surroundings. This  includes other vehicles’  shared
information, real-time traffic info, and pedestrians’ faces and
locations.
Furthermore,  in terms of security, ransomware, IoT attacks,
and DDoS, are such recent cyberattacks in addition to the more
classic ones that affect information and the operation of the
vehicle. Accordingly, safety systems need real-time detection
and accuracy from non-deterministic AI models with complex
computing on a combination of large amounts of data and
communication restrictions. These systems must accurately
function in a variety of weather  situations, with varying
visibility, and quality of the infrastructure and environment.
These requirements, though, can place a heavy real-time and
scale burden on these systems.
B. Local Data storing
Based on sensors and cameras positioned in various regions
both inside and outside the automobile, data is created and
maintained by the vehicle. According to how many ignition
cycles the car goes through, the majority of them will naintain
their data for a while before replacing it with fresh data.
Besides the data sent to the back-end servers and cloud, an
Event Data Recorder (EDR), and Data Storage System for
Automated Driving (DSSAD) are the main tools for storing
significant event data. The rest of  this section focuses on
these technologies, their functions, gaps, and current solutions
proposed.
1) EDR:  : A recording device inside the vehicle that can
capture information regarding an event [37] in a readily usable
manner. Its concept is the same for both AV and conventional
vehicles;  there is list of all  the vehicles that have EDR in
[38]. Such devices would support real crash investigations and
analyses of performance due to their data capturing of a few

seconds both pre- and post-crash. Useful and meaningful data
is stored, such as vehicle speed and brake status. For example,
the Tesla Model 3 (Autopilot) is designed to record data such
as: how various systems in the vehicle were operating; whether
or not  the driver and passenger safety belts were fastened;
how far (if at all) the driver was depressing the accelerator
and/or brake pedal; and, how fast the vehicle was travelling
[39]. Table III is an example of such recorded data in detail.
The technique behind this device generally stores data with
the airbag control module once it has been deployed. Other
recording techniques and event data recorders, which have been
explained comprehensively [37].
In December 1996,  the National Highway Traffic Safety
Administration (NHTSA) and the National Aeronautics Space
Administration (NASA) cooperated to improve airbag safety.
Consequently, NASA recommended that NHTSA encourage
installing and obtaining crash data for safety analyses from
crash recorders on vehicles  [37].  It  is worth  indicating,
however, that (EDR- AV) EDR for automated vehicles must
considerably exceed the performance of EDR for conventional
vehicles (EDR-CV) [40].

Publications around the world seek to
provide “guidelines for the development of automated driving
functions”, including identifying the technical requirements for
EDR-AV.
The Regulation (EU) 2019/2144 sets rules on technical require-
ments for new types of motor vehicles to maintain safety and
environmental protection [41]. According to that regulation, in
June 2022, the EDR is supposed to become mandatory for all
vehicles with SAE Level 3/Level 4 functions[40]. In addition,
The IWG EDR/DSSAD deals with the definition of the techni-
cal requirements as a prerequisite for the corresponding UN
regulations for both EDR systems (conventional/autonomous)
[42].
All crash data is required to be stored, be available, and
retrieved for crash reconstruction purposes, even for testing,
in order to understand the crash circumstances [43]. However,
for AV level 3 and above where the system can perform the
whole driving tasks for a certain time, EDR is unhelpful in
terms of responsibility and liability without the Data Storage
System for Automated Driving (DASSD) system to indicate
who was in control during the accident.
2) DASSD:  : A storage device that determines whether
the system or the driver has controlled the vehicle [44]. This
provides information regarding who was actually controlling
the driving task at a particular time in order to decide the
responsibility and consequently liability issues. Examples of
the stored data elements have been shown to be position and
time-stamped switches of the ADS from one mood to another
[45]. These may contain data regarding whether the system is
activated, manually, or automatically deactivated. Therefore, it
also records time and position, and when the driver is requested
to drive; position and time-stamped override through brake
control by the driver; position and time-stamped transition
demand by the ADS. Thus,  the nature of the reasons for a
transition demand or deactivation is able to be determined such
as:
• Driver not available or lack of driver attention.
• Driver override.

6

• System failure.
• Unplanned event (ex: bad weather).
While EDR is event-based storage, DASSD is thr continuous
storage of  specific AD data sets. All  this data shall  be
recognised without any possible confusion.
3) State of the art for traffic accident data records: The first
practical transportation data recorder was introduced in 1921.
It records vehicle speed, engine RPM and distance moved
onto a rotating circular chart [37]. Figure 6 shows the current
development movement of the existing national and regional
activities on this technology.
Despite the large role that these tools play in supporting
investigations, they have the following limitations:
1) Due  to storage  limitations and the  large number of
continuously variable parameters required for analysis.
EDR stores only specific data parameters for a specific
short time in specific events, basically events with airbag
deployment.
2) DSSAD is able to store a specific period, with older data
able to be erased when this storage is full [45].
3)  If the EDR system does not have sufficient space to record
an event, the current event data will overwrite the previous
event data.
4) 4) In addition, many accidents involving pedestrians and
cyclists,  in particular, are not recognised as significant
events, with no airbag deployed, meaning no event data
is stored. Even for the systems that activate EDR for
crashes (airbag deployment) or near-crash event (hitting
small object), there are events that are considered a crime;
however, the EDR indicates that it is an inefficient event,
such as a traffic offense [45]. This is useless from the
perspective of some regulators.

Fig. 6.

State of the art for traffic accident data records and their issues [45],[4
5) According to [40], There are five classes of data that
are typically required for  forensic analysis: firmware,
communication data, user data, safety-related data, and
security-related data which these devices lack.
6) Even continuous recording and storage of video, location,
speed, and/or surroundings of a vehicle appear  to be
contradictory with the regulations addressing privacy
protection. Therefore,  laws related to this matter need
to be enacted before it is adopted.
7) DSSAD would need consumers’ approval for recording
and/or access to data [45].
8) Manufacturers keep creating new generations of EDR
however, there is an urgent need for international stan-
dards.
The current Event Data Recording has to be refined to provide
sufficient data for liability purposes [46]. A survey in which
173 international  experts  in accident  analysis participated
depicted  that  a considerable number of  traffic accidents

7
4], and [2].
involving ADAS cannot be reconstructed [47]. According to
that paper, the main reason is that the information gathered
independently from the manufacturer is relatively poor. They
emphasise that the solution is a Forensic Event Data Recorder
(FEDR) which is an EDR that meets all the requirements from
the investigator’s perspective.
4) The way forward for EDR-AV/DSSAD: A number of
current attempts around the world aspire to correct the shortages
surrounding these technologies, including:
1) For automated vehicles, AHEAD recommends data record-
ing from 30 seconds before to 10 seconds after the colli-
sion. This time proves to be sufficient for the individual
EDR-relevant claims of the present claims collective [20].
2) As pedestrians and cyclists are  involved in a  lot of
accidents, the Netherlands intends to extend the scope of
the EDR with pedestrians and cyclists immediately.
3) governments, for example, in the UK, would apply a two-

step method. Firstly, to introduce the USA’s standards for
EDRs in a relatively short timeframe; then, the second
step applies more extensive requirements, if a single-step
approach were not possible [48].
4) Many researchers have proposed schemes for enhancing
the storage of EDR data, section IV-B6 discusses some.
5) The legal issues: The EDR data belongs to the owner
because the data describes driving and accident conditions
[49].  In the USA, ownership of the EDR data is a matter
of State law. However, NHTSA considers the owner of the
vehicle to be the owner of the data collected from an EDR.
In addition, courts are able to acquire EDR data through
court orders[43]. In addition to this ownership dilemma, the
accessibility of much of the data is another unstable issue. For
example,  for EDR data, some companies recently allowed
the owner to access all the EDR data by using some special
hardware and software, such as Tesla and BMW (until 2017
they were the only part that could reach it). Furthermore, the
original equipment manufacturer (OEM) may also access the
EDR remotely in some crash circumstances [39].
These issues represent obstacles in the way of accessing this
data by various stakeholders. Allowing stakeholders who may
have different purposes to access the data could be extremely
valuable. Thus, legislators, regulators, and manufacturers play
a significant role in forming what data should be recorded,
how the AI could select the useful data, how to increase the
record device size, how to access them, and who has the
ability to access them.
6)  Current Solutions:: Various safety analysis models,
including CAST [29] and FRAM [50], are used for accident
investigation. CAST employs system theory to identify failure
causes and propose preventive measures. FRAM assesses
complex interactions in socio-technical systems. These models
lack a unified framework covering all causal factors, and manual
analysis remains costly and inefficient.
Data integrity preservation for AVs has been a goal  for
several studies that have presented various frameworks based
on various forms of  technology for  investigation purposes.
Hoque and Hasan have also proposed a forensic investigation
framework for AVs; AVGuard tool [51] is supposed to be
integrated with the AD system. In addition, it assumes that
the AV has local storage to store the log provenance. The
AV can also communicate with a remote cloud server and
publish the newly created log provenance. A robot operating
system (ROS) node collects all the logs from different AD
modules. Oham et  al have proposed a distributed digital
forensics framework [52], which is based on the evidence
reported by the nearby witness AVs if an AV is involved in
an accident. Digital signatures, along with a corresponding
certificate are used to protect data integrity. Data exchanges
between entities in the framework are stored in a blockchain
and used for  later decision-making.

DIAT [53]  is another
Attestation framework, which preserves the integrity of data
generated by a program/device by ensuring the detection of
a large number of  run-time attacks based on control-flow
attestation.  It has a lower overhead relatively compared to
the other attestation-based solutions however it is not able to

8
detect sophisticated data-only attacks. Further, T-Box [54] is
a trusted real-time data recording system, which consists of
an automotive data recording system with a network monitor,
generator, and recorder. It assumes that the gateway could be
used as the network monitor. The generator reconstructs data
provided by the monitor and delivers it to the recorder which
in turn stores data. The recorder stores an individual data entry
into a block and these data blocks can be stored in local or
external storage or transmitted to a remote server. Even though
this could be considered a trusted automotive data recording
system that operates in real-time, however, it does not present
significant consideration for privacy issues. Buquerin et al [55]
pprovides a general concept for automotive forensics. Using
Ethernet, their implementation uses the on-board diagnostics
interface, the diagnostics over internet protocol, as well as the
unified diagnostic services for communication. Liu et al aim
to store EDR data safely and away from manipulation. In their
scheme [56], data is not only sent to the manufacturer’s server
as normal, but  the vehicle also uploads the EDR data to a
cloud server and sends the evidence of storage to the nearby
vehicle through a vehicular ad hoc network.
C. Local and Edge Processing
There are three approaches for processing in the cloud:
centralised location, edge-based processing offers flexibility;
and the Hybrid approach solution which is the most preferred.
The very  low-rate data are  regularly  transmitted  to  the
manufacturer’s  cloud where  all  processing occurs  at  a
centralised location. The high latency-sensitive data, however,
needs high-speed data processing distributed architecture. The
edge-based cloud data centre performs many tasks at  the
edge instead of the cloud leading to faster access than cloud
computing. Edge and Fog computing plays a critical role here
for the data require high network bandwidth, which provides
data processing and local storage capabilities [57].
Even though clouds are crucial  for  the success of AVs,
there  are  several  challenges  facing  the vehicular  cloud
community that are not facing traditional cloud computing.
In  addition  to  the  privacy,  security,  authorisation,  and
authentication issues in any cloud computing,  the dynamic
resource allocation in the vehicles and the lack of a central
authority make addressing these issues more challenging[58],
[59]. In this era, several studies have been provided in table IV.
TABLE IV
TABLE OF MOBILE EDGE COMPUTING (MEC) SOLUTION STUDIES

computation time  energy consumption  resource  privacy

D. Backend Cloud Computing
Most  companies  acknowledge and state  through  their
official websites that  they keep a lot of data regarding the
vehicle owner, the vehicle itself, and its in-vehicle hardware
and software products [39]. There is a great current dilemma
that requires research and balance about how much of the
data must be stored on-board versus instantly dismissed. The
analyst Christian Renaud in a talk with Blocks & Files states
that 78% of OEMs plan to have between 50-70 % of all
that data analysed on the vehicles, while the rest are sent off
the vehicle [67]. The period of retention of this data varies
from one company to another.  It also differs  in the way
of obtaining this data, some transfer them to the back-end
servers physically, and others transfer them remotely through
networks.  In addition,  they differ  in the format of data;
companies such as Tesla use raw sensor data, whereas some
other companies ask Original Design Manufacturing(ODM)
to provide processed data. Captured and stored data requires
large storage architecture; for instance Cloud, or on-premises
servers. The majority of automakers are utilizing cloud-based
capabilities via connected-car services. For example, in 2019,
Ford publicised about  its connected-vehicle collaboration
with AWS.  In addition, Toyota announced its engineering
ecosystem to develop and deploy the next generation of its
cloud-connected vehicle in 2020. These examples and others
are available in [68].
Indeed, in a promising attempt to address the data management
challenges in AVs, Cloud technology is the scalable solution
in the automotive industry. Therefore,  this different source
data is being classified to be onboard or sent  to the cloud
or stored in servers and hard drives as long-term storage.
Local function data and V2X data that has a 4ms response
time requirement and has to go off-vehicle will not be sent
to clouds [67].

In contrast, model training data, for example,
includes when a new object has been detected, and the data
regarding this disparity will be used to formulate patterns and
reports, and thus, will be sent to the cloud for future algorithm
improvements [69], [70].
E. The Need for Data Sharing
Even though some vehicle manufacturers accept to share in-
vehicle data with the other service providers by accessing
data directly through the vehicle manufacturer’s server or
via “neutral” servers that would gather  the data. However,
the service providers ask for direct  real-time access to in-
vehicle-produced data and functions through an in-vehicle
interoperable, standardised, secure, and open-access platform
[71]. Besides any law party, Tesla states that the data could be
shared with their service providers, business partners, and
affiliates;  in addition to any third parties,  the owner has
also been authorised[39]. Some stakeholders suggest that car
manufacturers allow tier 1 suppliers to access the data directly
[72] to maintain and improve their products. Figure 7 illustrates
the levels of tiers of any manufacturer.
Various situations are defined in the literature where different
stakeholders need to access accurate AV data such as causes of

9
Fig. 7.  The Levels of Tiers of Any Manufacturer.
accidents and failure data. In traditional vehicles, human error
is the critical cause of 94% of vehicle accidents [73]. However,
in the case of an accident with a self-driving car, there are
clear differences in liability. In particular, the interaction of
many factors in AV and its ability to make some or all driving
decisions disrupt the adjudication process. The liability subjects
of AV accidents may include vehicles, vehicle assistant drivers,
manufacturers, vehicle owners, and insurance companies [74].
In the literature, authors and organisations differed in the
identification of stakeholders and their techniques to identify.
NHTSA states that motor vehicle accidents may be investigated
through various entities, and they listed them [37]. In another
example in the article [9], the authors fragmented stakeholders
into three general classes: A) containing all types of end-users
and society; B) containing all technical groups; and C) for
all regulatory parties including insurers. However, the most
frequent entities in literature are as the following:
1) Government and the legal authority: The accident data
collected from the involved AV are necessary as facts and
evidence in criminal courts to determine liability. Another
possible scenario for government use is to take advantage
of data to reduce the possibility of accidents in the future
and thus reduce losses and assess roadside safety.
2) Original Equipment Manufacturer(OEM): The Manufac-
turers may analyse the collected data and use it to monitor
system performance and improve their products. Another
possible scenario is to reconstruct the accident to assess
the causes in order to reduce the possibility of accidents
in the future.
3) Suppliers: The potential scenario is to analyse and improve
the products or services that are involved in a vehicle
manufactured by the OEM.
4) The owner: In a possible scenario the owner might use
the data as evidence to absolve him/her of any criminal
liability. In addition, the provided data could be used for
insurance amounts and services.
5) Insurance: The stored data present accurate evidence to
resolve insurance disputes and a fair solution that ensures
that no party is tampered with.
6) The testing and certification: the testing organisation needs
the data to reconstruct the accident in order to analyse
and update the technical and legal requirements.
7) Road authorities: Data can support assessing and improv-
ing the infrastructure and roadside safety.
8) Researchers: To analyse and assess the AV crashes to
assist in the development of vehicles, infrastructure, and

the whole environment’s components.
Due to the significance of saving the data received and issued
by this system, it is clear that sharing this data with all relevant
parties is essential.
Secure multi-party data sharing is not a brand-new area
of study.  In the literature, several authors propose sharing
data frameworks confidentially and integrally. One approach
to mitigate privacy concerns is by using Federated Learning
(FL), which is a distributed collaborative AI approach that
allows multiple devices to coordinate data training with a
central server without sharing actual datasets [75]. With a
trusted server (aggregator), parties can learn a shared machine
learning model locally and separately, as well as share only
the resulting insights from each analysis. This technology is an
active era adopted recently in various applications and research.
In financial  application, Byrd and Polychroniadou [76]
proposed federated learning with differential privacy and
secure multiparty computation to a problem of collective
interest in finance.

As one of the main limitations of FL is
the reconstruction attacks and re-identification from correlated
data sets, they apply Differential Privacy, which adds noise
to the input data set. What  is for mobile applications,  a
protocol has been proposed by Bonawitz et al. for FL [77].
Even though their secure aggregation protocol  resulted in
high communication costs,  it proved that the central server
could not see any local updates. Sotthiwat et al. propose a
partially encrypted Multi-Party Computation (MPC) solution
by encrypting only the critical parts of model parameters
to reduce local computation and communication costs.  In
particular,  their work only encrypts the first  layer of local
models with MPC strategy [78].  In biomedical  research,
the FAMHE framework relies on Multiparty Homomorphic
Encryption (MHE) to perform privacy-preserving FA by using
the advantages of both interactive protocols and homomorphic
encryption HE [79]. The approach enables several sites to
compute their local patient-level data and then encrypt and
homomorphically combine  their  local  results under MHE
Collective Aggregation (CA). The collectively encrypted and
aggregated final result is eventually switched from encryption
under  the collective public key  to encryption under  the
querier’s public key; thus, only the querier can decrypt.
Several Blockchain-based FL designs have been provided
recently. For example, [80] is a distributed on-vehicle machine
learning model  that  aims  to improve vehicular networks.
Data could be trained and models could be exchanged in
a distributed manner. Zeng et  al  [81]  also designed an
autonomous controller to avoid performance deficiencies of
traditional learning-based controllers that are trained by each
connected vehicle’s local data. In their design,  the learning
models used by the controllers are collaboratively trained
among a group of CAVs.
Data management and security are part of Blockchain’s
most applied research directions for transportation. Based on
Blockchain, BIIoVT is a secure storage architecture proposed
to ensure the security, confidentiality, and privacy of data stored
in the cloud [82]. It assumes that every vehicular network has
one Blockchain for distributed secure infrastructure. All city

10
vehicular networks transferred the secure data to a distributed
hash table, which is used at the cloud layer. To address the
same gap, Kang et  al.  establish a secure and distributed
vehicular blockchain for data management in vehicular edge
computing and network to achieve secure data sharing among
vehicles [83]. In addition, Jain et al present A comprehensive
survey regarding recent advances in autonomous vehicles and
systems and how blockchain is able to help in improving user
experiences and improving industry practices [84].
1) Data validation: An AD system generates a considerable
amount of data, making the extraction of crucial information
from the logs of different AD modules a real challenge. In
case of a defect, finding the set of influencing factors causing
the failure in an automated system is a complex mission.
There are two reasons behind this complexity [85]: firstly,
the AD functions may not be sufficient for all unexpected
conditions, due to the dynamic environment with unlimited
contexts; secondly deviation from the intended functionality
due to the inductive nature of a system that combines machine
learning components. Nested and complex factors of functional
insufficiency could lead to an unintended result. Failure in
sensor fusion or sensor’s reading (e.g.: misdetection of the
front vehicle), external environment context (e.g.: weather), or
control failure (ex: braking is not initiated in time), or even
a combination of some or all of these factors (e.g.: a glare
causes misdetection of a pedestrian implying that braking is
not initiated in time) are potential factors in critical scenarios
for Automated Driving[85], [86]. Within the enormous amount
Fig. 8.  The Complexity of Finding The Source of Harm[85].
of produced data, searching for meaningful data that could
lead to the crash causes, is immensely difficult to locate. All
the generated data must be valid and classified as (useful – not
useful) before any analysing process. To avoid the accident,
it is important to persist with the high-quality training data
sets that are used for AI systems that assist the vehicle to be
able to make decisions [87]. Finding the exact moment of the
failure, the exact wrong data, device, or subsystem is a real
obstacle. Figure 8 above explicates the complexity of finding
the source of harm.
Furthermore, as crashes are rare events, testing and analysing
similar scenarios to find the exact harm that may blame one
party or another in this nested system is a real challenge. In
2011 the automotive standard of regulation of the International
Standardisation Organization (ISO) ISO 26262:2011 for the.

A. Data Privacy
AV is equipped and will continue to improve the tracing
technology to understand failure circumstances  for many
purposes. However, these forms of technologiy have a serious
impact on not only users’ privacy but also on pedestrians
whose photos and locations can be captured by vehicles. As
the generated data in AV is collected, stored, transferred, and
shared, users’ privacy could be extremely violated in the
following faces:
1) User’s information: Identifying information regarding the
user of the vehicle may be collected and maintained for
many purposes for different parties.
2) Sensors data The sensors collect data about whatever they
see or sense. Cameras could capture people’s identities
and their locations.
3) Location The combination of location data and driver data
could provide sensitive private information [24].
4) Cloud Data The cloud stores several private data such as
driver profiles, car maintenance data, destination data, and
financial information [90]
The open wireless access in VANET leads to serious security
and privacy issues. For example, [91] is a paper containing
survey papers on privacy,  security,  and authentication  in
VANETs and the surveys of papers addressing these issues.
Thr design in[92] demonstrates an efficient privacy-preserving
authentication scheme for V2G networks to ensure that electric
vehicles (EVs) securely access services provided by the grid.
5G radio network architecture is also proposed [93], which
leverages multiple radio access technologies and utilises Cloud
Radio Access Network (CRAN) functionalities for privacy-
preserved and secure CAV networks. To protect the location
and trajectory privacy of Vehicular Social Networks (VSNs)
based on 5G,  [94] design a framework that  incorporates
Mobile Femtocell  technology. They propose the Dynamic
Group Division algorithm (DGD) that increases the likelihood
of exchanging pseudo-nyms. Nonetheless, the privacy issue is
one of the concerns associated with all phases of data flow in
AV and must be investigated.
B. Security
Security is a key issue in AVs as any network attack could
eventually cost lives. Based on the components of AVs, the
attacks could be classified as:
In-vehicle network

11
Fig. 9. Attack Taxonomy [98].
Attacks include:
1) ECU engine control units. An attacker can modify the
programming code during design and implementation
processing [95]
2) CAN and SEA J1939 busses: Security issues involving
the CAN bus, which connects to all the vehicle’s compo-
nents. A virus could modify the messages delivered by
the controller area network (CAN) bus [95]. In [96] the
authors developed a mobile application that can control
and manipulate such vehicle systems remotely through
the Internet.
3) Remote sensors: External entities can make connections
with sensors through existing wireless networks; therefore
the attacker may implement remote sensor control [97].
Any tampering with the sensors’ data generated and
transmitted is able to result in fatal accidents.
4) GPS: This provides location and time information, which
is  the core of many functions,  such as pseudonym
certificates, basic safety messages, and timestamps in
messages [97]. The adversary may modify the code to
report incorrect GPS location data [95].
5) Wireless communication: Vulnerabilities may occur by
Bluetooth, tire pressure monitoring system (TPMS), or
keyless entry and ignition systems[97].
Vehicle to everything network (V2X)
Attacks on V2X typically target:
1) VALNET or Vehicle ad-hoc networks which use dedicated
short-range communications (DSRC) and are based on the
IEEE 802.11p standard for wireless access in vehicular
environments.
2) Another communication structure required for V2X is the
mobile cellular network, satellite radio, and Bluetooth
VANET demonstrates the need to address various security
and privacy issues. Mansour et al [94] presented the essential
security and privacy requirements in VANET; while Gupta et
al [98] have provided a taxonomy, based on the architecture
of hardware, network, and software (see figure 9).
Several solutions in the vehicular network have been recently
proposed. To provide confidentiality and authenticity, Hu Xiong
et al [99] designed an identity-based (ID-based) encryption
scheme. RTED-SD is a scheme that was developed to detect
real-time attacks. In their scheme [100], they use the Fast Quar-
tile Deviation Check algorithm (FQDC) is used to recognise and

In light of the emergence of the 4.0 industry, self-driving cars
are considered at the forefront of this revolution. On one side, it
provides numerous advantages regarding the environment and
road safety. However, very few people would still have enough
trust to use those vehicles. This massive amount of data is
both extremely important and dangerous, making it extremely
delicate to use at this stage. On the one hand, it can be used
to extract a wealth of valuable information in order to improve
the transportation system and increase safety. Comparatively, if
not properly preserved, it may cost us our lives. Beginning with
remote-control door locks, hackers may load firmware without
authorisation into major ECUs and progress to sensor-based
manipulation of cooperative adaptive cruise control (CACC).
In addition, user data could be collected specific way, as the
GM collects location data and radio listening routines from
its users [115]. Due to the open nature of the used networks,
safety, security, and privacy become vital issues
